metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'e.g.' alternatives with a single decided command and its parameter values (e.g. state the chosen default: `docker builder prune -f --filter until=72h && docker image prune -f`).
findings.finding: The specific prune command and parameters are left as illustrative alternatives ('e.g. docker builder prune -f --keep-storage cap OR --filter until=72h'), deferring a real design decision to the executor rather than stating a chosen default.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'Run `docker builder prune -f --filter until=72h && docker image prune -f` on the failure path (same command style as the existing success-path prune at line 166).' This commits to a specific, reviewable default.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'e.g.' flag options with a single, concrete decision (specific `--keep-storage` value or `--filter` expression) so that 'bounded build-cache growth' in the AC is quantified and testable.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'e.g.' for the prune flags (`--keep-storage` cap or `--filter until=72h`), leaving the actual storage bound unspecified; this means the AC condition 'bounded build-cache growth' has no quantified, testable bound.
findings.suggested_fix: Choose one approach and commit to it, e.g.: 'Use `docker builder prune -f --filter until=24h` on the failure path; the success-path prune remains unchanged. Rationale: 24h bound limits cache to at most one day of failed-build accumulation on a 30G disk.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the ambiguity in AC1: either restrict the described approach to `--keep-storage` (which enforces a hard bound) or rewrite AC1 to say 'prune executed on failure path' without the word 'bounded', so both described options are valid implementations.
findings.finding: AC1 uses the word 'bounded' ('leaves bounded build-cache growth') implying a hard storage cap, but the What section offers two approaches — `--keep-storage` cap (which does bound storage) and `--filter until=72h` (time-based, which does NOT strictly bound growth under rapid failure loops). This terminology drift between the criterion and the described implementation options could send an implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 vs. What section
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC1 to: 'A failed deploy attempt triggers cache prune on the failure path (e.g. via --keep-storage cap or --filter until=72h), preventing unbounded accumulation.' Then in the What section, note that --keep-storage provides a hard bound while --filter until=72h provides a time-based bound, and pick one or document the trade-off.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g. for AC1: `bash -c 'source autodeploy.sh; simulate_failure_path; docker system df'` or a bats test asserting the prune command appears in failure-path output; for AC2: the existing test suite invocation; for AC3: the exact test file path and run command).
findings.finding: No concrete proving command is specified for any of the three acceptance criteria; in particular, AC1's end-to-end exercise of the failure path (actually triggering a failed deploy and confirming the prune ran) is entirely absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Restate AC1 with a single, specific observable: either 'the prune command is present on the failure path (verified by grep/bats)' OR 'disk used by buildkit layers after a failed deploy does not exceed Y GB (verified by docker system df)'.
findings.finding: AC1 states 'bounded build-cache growth' without specifying the bound, making it impossible to evaluate pass/fail against a measurable threshold.
findings.suggested_fix: Preferred minimal fix: 'A failed deploy attempt executes `docker builder prune` and `docker image prune` on the failure path, verified by a shell test asserting the commands are called when the build step exits non-zero.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `docker builder prune` should also be added to (or already covers) the success path at line 166, given that BuildKit layer accumulation — not just dangling images — was the root cause of Incident 2731.
findings.finding: `docker builder prune` (which the plan's 'What' section proposes to address buildkit layer accumulation) does not currently exist anywhere in autodeploy.sh — not on the success path, not on the failure path. The plan adds it only to the failure path, creating an unacknowledged asymmetry: builder cache still accumulates unbounded on the success path. The plan's Scope says 'keep the success-path prune' (singular, implying no change), but never acknowledges that the success-path prune (`docker image prune -f`, line 166) does NOT purge the BuildKit cache that caused the incident.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note or AC explicitly stating whether the success-path prune at line 166 should be extended to `docker image prune -f && docker builder prune -f [--keep-storage X | --filter until=72h]`, or justify why success-path builder cache is bounded without it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the prune call on the failure path swallows its own exit code (e.g. `|| true`) so it cannot mask the deploy failure, AND that its outcome (bytes freed or error) is logged for operator observability.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how a failure of the prune command itself should be handled on the failure path — whether its exit code should be swallowed (to preserve the original deploy-failure exit code and avoid masking the root cause) or surfaced, and there is no mention of logging/instrumentation for the prune invocation's outcome.
findings.suggested_fix: Add something like: `docker builder prune -f --filter until=72h 2>&1 | logger -t autodeploy-prune || true` on the failure path, and document that prune errors are non-fatal but logged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that on the health-check failure path (where the build SUCCEEDED but /health failed), `docker image prune -f` does NOT remove the newly tagged `:latest`/`:prev` images; specify whether to also add `docker rmi` of the specific failed image tags, use `docker image prune -a --filter until=1h`, or accept this residual as bounded (explain why).
findings.finding: On the health-check failure path (line 159), `docker compose build` succeeded and the new image is stored as a fully-tagged `:latest` (and `:prev`) image. `docker image prune -f` only removes DANGLING (untagged) images, so these tagged layers are NOT pruned. Under repeated deploy-loop churn where source changes each cycle (e.g. a flaky health endpoint), tagged image layers still accumulate across failure cycles, leaving the 'bounded build-cache growth' acceptance criterion only partially satisfied.
findings.location: ## What / Acceptance Criteria line 1; maps to autodeploy.sh:148-159
findings.suggested_fix: On the health-check failure path specifically, after the rollback, consider also running `docker rmi "${BOT_IMAGE}:latest" 2>/dev/null || true` (safe because :prev has already been re-tagged to :latest before rollback) or use `docker image prune -a --filter until=1h -f` to catch recently-built untagged layers. Document the residual tagged-image bound explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `--keep-storage` cap (or `--filter until` value) as a named shell variable or env-var at the top of `autodeploy.sh` (e.g. `BUILDER_PRUNE_KEEP=${BUILDER_PRUNE_KEEP:-10g}`) so it is discoverable and overridable without editing script internals.
findings.finding: The `--keep-storage` cap (or `--filter until=72h`) threshold is a tunable value that will be hardcoded in the shell script. As disk capacity or deploy frequency changes, the threshold will need updating, but the plan provides no mechanism (env var, config constant, or comment) to make it discoverable or adjustable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `BUILDER_PRUNE_KEEP=${BUILDER_PRUNE_KEEP:-10g}` near the top of `autodeploy.sh` and reference it in the prune call: `docker builder prune -f --keep-storage "$BUILDER_PRUNE_KEEP"`. Document the variable and its rationale in a comment citing Incident 2731.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the `--keep-storage` example to include a concrete size value (e.g. `--keep-storage 5gb`) or replace it with the time-based `--filter until=72h` alternative, so the implementer does not copy a syntax-error invocation.
findings.finding: The plan mentions `docker builder prune -f --keep-storage` as an example option but `--keep-storage` requires a mandatory size argument (e.g. `--keep-storage 5gb`); as written it is a syntax error that would cause the prune command to fail silently (swallowed by `|| true`).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `docker builder prune -f --keep-storage` with `docker builder prune -f --filter until=72h` (time-bound, no required argument, directly matches the 30 G disk/loop scenario) or `docker builder prune -f --keep-storage 5gb`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the plan (e.g. 'inline in the failure branches preserves testability via shell tests, keeps failure-path logic co-located, and avoids prune on no-op timer fires') to satisfy the G6 rationale requirement.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing the inline-in-script approach over structurally different alternatives (e.g. a systemd ExecStopPost hook that always prunes, or a separate periodic prune timer). Without a stated rationale, implementers have no guidance for future related decisions and reviewers cannot confirm the choice was deliberate.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope — no rationale section present

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `docker builder prune --help` on the target host (i-00880b2c7f13527c5) to confirm `--keep-storage` and `--filter until=72h` flag names match the installed Docker version, and record the exact invocation in the plan.
findings.finding: `docker builder prune --keep-storage` flag name is Partially-verified: the plan names this flag but it cannot be confirmed against live `docker builder prune --help` output in this environment. If the installed Docker version spells or positions the flag differently (e.g. some versions use `--keep-storage` while others may differ), the command silently does nothing or errors out at runtime on the failure path — exactly the path that must work under disk pressure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-liner in the plan or a script comment: `docker builder prune --help | grep -E 'keep-storage|filter'` run on the target box, with the observed output pasted into the PR, so the exact flag form is locked before merge.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
